items.text: An activity is declared once as a typed signature plus a tier annotation (InVm | RemotePython | RemoteRust) in the activities module, and that declaration is the only per-activity artifact an author writes.

`aion generate` derives, from the typed declaration, the activity.new wrapper, the value-type codec pairs, the worker handler stub, the worker registration entry, and the workflow.toml activities entry.
`aion generate` derives the wire-compat golden for each remote activity from the value-type codecs, so the worker/SDK wire shapes are pinned without a hand-derived literal.
`aion generate --check` regenerates every generated file in memory and exits non-zero if any on-disk generated file differs, so a hand-edit to generated output is a build failure.

Generated activity configuration carries no invented defaults: a retry policy, timeout, or backoff appears in generated code only when the author declared it, otherwise the required hole remains required (ADR-001).
Deleting every generated file for a worked example (e.g. order-saga) and running `aion generate` reproduces them byte-identical, proven by a round-trip test.
`aion dev` watches a package and on save rebuilds, repackages, and hot-reloads the new content-hash version without restarting the engine.
The dev UI triggers a workflow run and streams that run's events live over the existing WebSocket event stream.
The dev UI replays a failed run and lets the author mock a named activity's result on an opt-in basis for a given run.
The dev loop runs the real engine, store, and event stream — there is no mock-only execution path whose semantics diverge from production (CN4).
An end-to-end test edits a workflow under `aion dev` and observes the new version serve a fresh run with no engine restart.
The aion-toolchain crate compiles and type-checks Gleam workflow source by shelling out to the gleam binary and packages a .aion on success, embedding no compiler.
aion-server exposes authoring endpoints, gated on --gleam-path, that accept Gleam source and return the gleam type error inline on a compile failure.
On a successful compile the server packages via aion-toolchain and hot-loads the new version, making authoring a live loop against the running engine.
Without --gleam-path the server deploys pre-built .aion files only and carries no compiler dependency (CN7).
A run's history is navigable event-by-event in the dashboard, reading the existing event store with no second debug log (CN5).
At each event the debugger shows the workflow-visible state projection and the recorded now() and random() values for that step.
On a NonDeterminismError the debugger surfaces the exact divergent command (expected vs found at the sequence) the resolver already computes.
The debugger offers a what-if re-run from a chosen event with a mocked outcome via the existing aion/testing replay path.
The per-event state projection is exposed by the engine from history and replay, not maintained as a parallel mutable store.
A workflow's primitive structure (run / spawn / receive / all / race / sleep and control flow) is extracted from the package and rendered to a graph automatically.
A live run overlays its progress on the same graph, lighting the active node as the engine emits events.
Rendering an example and diffing the node/edge set against the workflow's known structure matches, proving the diagram is generated from the source rather than hand-drawn.
A bounded structural edit on the canvas regenerates Gleam that still type-checks; the canvas is never the authoritative artifact (CN6).

sections.name: Agentic authoring and the determinism gate (WA-006, WA-007)

items.text: `aion new agent` scaffolds a durable agent loop (scout -> act -> verify -> signal-gated review) parameterised by prompts + schemas + gate, generalising the stacked-dev shape.
The agent scaffold compiles and runs a trivial agent end-to-end, including a human-in-the-loop signal wait with a timeout.
The agent scaffold's human approval pause is a workflow.receive with a timeout, not a bespoke polling mechanism.
`aion check --deterministic` statically flags any wall-clock or entropy call reachable from workflow code and passes a clean workflow, proven by a positive and a negative fixture.
`aion generate` emits an aion/testing skeleton per workflow: each activity pre-mocked, a clock advance per timer, and a replay-determinism assertion.
`aion input <workflow_type>` emits a valid input skeleton derived from the workflow's input type, so an input document is never hand-written from scratch.
